title: Second Encounter
text_warrior:
She finds you at the edge of the wolf-road, in the hour before dawn when the fire is low and the watch is yours alone.

She does not freeze the others this time. They sleep. You will check, after, and they will not remember waking.

She comes without announcement — one moment the tree line is dark and the next she is standing at the fire's edge, watching it as though she has always been here and it is you who arrived late.

"It's settling," she says, without turning. She means the mark. You know this without being told. "Faster than I expected. You've been fighting hard." A pause. Something in her voice that is not quite admiration. "I find that very interesting."

She turns then, and the fire catches her the same way it always does — the light knows what it's illuminating. Her eyes go to your sternum, briefly, before returning to your face. Reading something. Satisfied with what she finds.

"You'll begin to notice changes," she says. "Small ones first. You already have — you're just calling them something else." She tilts her head, a fraction. "Stop doing that. Call them what they are. It goes easier."

She does not explain what goes easier.

She does not need to.
text_scholar:
She finds you at the edge of the wolf-road, in the hour before dawn when the fire is low and the watch is yours alone.

She does not freeze the others this time. They sleep. You will check, after, and they will not remember waking.

She comes without announcement — one moment the tree line is dark and the next she is standing at the fire's edge, watching it as though she has always been here and it is you who arrived late.

"It's settling," she says, without turning. She means the mark. You know this without being told. "Faster than I expected. You've been documenting the changes carefully." Something in her voice that might be approval. "That's useful. Keep doing it."

She turns then, and the firelight catches her with its full attention. Her eyes go to your sternum, briefly, before returning to your face.

"Your notes on the corruption's preferred direction," she says, as though continuing a conversation you began. "You're right, by the way. It isn't diffuse. It has a destination." She pauses. "You."

She does not explain this.

"You'll begin to notice changes," she says instead. "You're already noticing. You're just framing them as external phenomena rather than internal ones. Stop doing that. The methodology is wrong."

She does not tell you what the correct methodology is. She does not need to. You already know.
text_scout:
She finds you at the edge of the wolf-road, in the hour before dawn when the fire is low and the watch is yours alone.

She does not freeze the others this time. They sleep. You will check, after, and they will not remember waking.

You heard her approach. Or thought you did — a pressure change, a wrongness in the air, the same signature as a portal forming but smaller. You were turned toward her before she was visible.

She notices this. Something in her expression shifts, almost imperceptibly.

"You read it," she says, standing at the fire's edge. Not quite a question. "The approach. Most don't." A pause. "I find that very interesting."

She turns then, and the firelight does what firelight does with her — cooperates completely. Her eyes go to your sternum, briefly, before returning to your face.

"It's settling," she says. "The mark. Faster than I expected, given how hard you've been resisting." She tilts her head. "Stop reading the changes as threats. You're misclassifying them."

She does not tell you what to classify them as instead. She doesn't need to. You've been doing it already, in the quiet hours, when you think no one is watching.

She was watching.
text_default:
She finds you at the edge of the wolf-road, in the hour before dawn when the fire is low and the watch is yours alone.

She does not freeze the others this time. They sleep. You will check, after, and they will not remember waking.

She comes without announcement — one moment the tree line is dark and the next she is standing at the fire's edge, watching the coals as though she has always been here.

"It's settling," she says, without turning. She means the mark. You know this without being told. "Faster than I expected."

She turns then, and the firelight catches her the same way it always does. Her eyes go to your sternum, briefly, before returning to your face.

"You'll begin to notice changes," she says. "Small ones first. You already have." A pause. "Call them what they are. It goes easier."

She does not explain what goes easier.

She does not need to.

companion_reactions.saoirse: Saoirse says nothing at morning camp. She watches the tree line while she eats. Later, unprompted, she moves her bedroll to the side of camp nearest the forest.
companion_reactions.mireille: Mireille writes a full page before she speaks. 'She's coming back at intervals,' she says finally. 'That implies a schedule. Schedules imply intent.' She underlines 'intent' three times.
companion_reactions.tierlan: Tierlan spends the morning checking snares that don't need checking. He does not ask what kept you awake.

aftermath: The morning breaks clear and cold. You are aware of the mark in a way you weren't before — not pain, but attention. As though something has started paying closer notice to the work it began.
